toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. ## Developer Complaint
2. 
3. Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
4. for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
5. while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
6. 
7. We've already verified:
8. - No analyzers are slowing things down
9. - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
10. - The project itself compiles in <500ms
11. 
12. The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
13. project graph on every CLI invocation.
14. 
15. We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
16. command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.
17.

 ## Developer Complaint
 
 Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
 for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
 while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
 
 We've already verified:
 - No analyzers are slowing things down
 - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
 - The project itself compiles in <500ms
 
 The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
 project graph on every CLI invocation.
 
 We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
 command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.

 ## Developer Complaint
 
 Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
 for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
 while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
 
 We've already verified:
 - No analyzers are slowing things down
 - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
 - The project itself compiles in <500ms
 
 The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
 project graph on every CLI invocation.
 
 We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
 command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.

data.content:
## Analysis

The developer report nails the root cause: **MSBuild process startup and project re-evaluation overhead on every CLI invocation**. Visual Studio avoids this because it keeps MSBuild loaded in-process. The CLI spawns a new MSBuild process each time by default.

Here are the key fixes, in order of impact:

### 1. Enable the MSBuild Server (biggest win)

This keeps an MSBuild process resident between builds — the same optimization VS uses. Add to the `.csproj`:

arguments.new_str:
<PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Reuse a long-lived MSBuild server process across builds to avoid
       cold-start overhead on every CLI invocation (similar to Visual Studio). -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildingInsideVisualStudio)' != 'true'">
    <MSBuildServer>true</MSBuildServer>
  </PropertyGroup>

data.reasoningText:
The developer reports that `dotnet build` CLI builds are slow (2-4 seconds) for incremental builds even when nothing has changed, while Visual Studio rebuilds nearly instantly.

The key insight here is that Visual Studio uses an MSBuild server process that stays resident in memory, avoiding the cold-start cost of loading MSBuild, evaluating the project graph, and JIT-compiling MSBuild code on every invocation.

Starting with .NET 8 SDK (and available experimentally since .NET 7), there's an MSBuild Server feature that keeps the MSBuild process alive between builds, similar to what Visual Studio does. This can be enabled by setting the environment variable `DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1` or by adding `<MSBuildServer>true</MSBuildServer>` to the project/Directory.Build.props.


Beyond the MSBuild server, there are a few other optimizations worth considering: using the `--no-restore` flag to skip NuGet restore when dependencies haven't changed, managing the build server lifetime with `dotnet build-server`, and enabling `<GenerateFullPaths>true</GenerateFullPaths>` in Directory.Build.props for better IDE integration. The MSBuild server is really the main lever here for addressing the performance gap.

data.content:
## Summary of recommendations

**Applied — `<MSBuildServer>true</MSBuildServer>`** (in the `.csproj`):  
Keeps an MSBuild worker process alive between `dotnet build` invocations, eliminating the 1–3 second cold-start tax (process launch, JIT, project-graph evaluation). This is the same mechanism that makes Visual Studio feel instant. The `Condition` ensures it only applies to CLI builds.

**Additional tips for the dev inner loop:**

| Technique | Effect |
|---|---|
| `dotnet build --no-restore` | Skips NuGet restore check (~200-400 ms) when packages haven't changed |
| `dotnet watch build` | Watches for file changes and rebuilds automatically with the process kept alive |
| Set env var `DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1` | Machine-wide alternative to the per-project property — useful if you have many repos |

The MSBuild Server property in the project file is the most impactful single change and should bring CLI incremental builds close to Visual Studio speed.
